# ‘My debts haunt me like furies’: Mary Wollstonecraft and the feminism of her personal letters

Laura Brace <https://orcid.org/0000-0002-0835-7448> lb21@le.ac.uk View all authors and affiliations

OnlineFirst

<https://doi.org/10.1177/14647001251318206>

## Abstract

By analysing Mary Wollstonecraft's personal letters to her friends, her siblings and her lover, this article finds new ways of exploring how property informs her ideas about freedom and independence, and of connecting her ideas to our present. Her personal letters allow us to start a new conversation with Wollstonecraft, and to rethink the intimate connections between ideas and lives which have been so contested in past interpretations of her political thought. Drawing on vulnerable feminist methods, the article focuses on three moments of disruption, ambivalence and failure, namely her experiences of losing her inheritance; of trying to extricate her sister from her marriage; and of being abandoned by her lover. In these moments, as well as in her experience of a life ‘involved with debts’, we can trace how property functioned as a system of power in Wollstonecraft's life and in the lives of those around her. By reading her very personal letters with compassion, the article opens up a dialogue about mental health, grief, care, indebtedness and dependence that enriches our present feminism and speaks very directly to current concerns. Wollstonecraft's letters offer a rich resource for exploring her political theory. Through close reading of the collected letters written between 1773 and 1797, this article argues for a novel understanding of Wollstonecraft's conceptions of freedom and independence, drawing on her ideas of composure and waywardness, and the notion of being settled. The preoccupations and concerns of her letters, the traumas and the vulnerabilities that they expose, forcefully illustrate the gendered-ness of property, and of freedom, both in her times and in ours. They contribute to the feminist project of expanding the meaning of politics, helping us to think about property, power and the homing desire in the space between the public and private.

## Returning home and going forward

Mary Wollstonecraft used to sleep outside her mother's bedroom to protect her from the violence of her husband, and wrote in a letter to her friend Jane Arden that ‘It is almost needless to tell you that my father's violent temper and extravagant turn of mind, was the principal cause of my unhappiness and that of the rest of the family’ (To Jane Arden, early 1780, in Todd, 2003 : 23). What if we start trying to understand Wollstonecraft's political ideas from here, in this moment and through her personal letters? In studying Wollstonecraft's letters to her friends, her siblings and her lover, we can find a new way of exploring how property is ‘sewn into’ her ideas about freedom and independence as well as her republicanism ( Halldenius, 2019 : 388), and of connecting her ideas to our present. By bringing in the context of her own indebtedness and the struggles of her life, we can deepen our understandings of property as a contested moral space, and of what it means for women to live and survive as independent beings in civil society. Reading her letters gives us access to Wollstonecraft as a complex figure, especially as we move her from past to present and uncover some of the commonalities that are derived from historically variable experience ( Brah, 2022 : 71). I explore her place in women's property-history, the extent to which women were able to determine and act on their own interests, to maintain their expectations and to use property as a buffer against the irregularity and caprice of others ( Brace, 2007 ). In this way, I aim to start a new conversation with Wollstonecraft's political theory by bringing in her family and friends, and a sense of her ‘home’.

Wollstonecraft wrote hundreds of letters from the age of 14 until she died at the age of 38; a total of 354 of them are published in the definitive edition of her correspondence edited by Janet Todd in 2003. Todd traced the provenance of each letter, and left them ‘largely as written’ ( Todd, 2003 : xxx), bringing her readers as close to the original manuscripts (held in the Lord Abinger Collection at the Bodleian Library, Oxford and elsewhere) as possible. The letters, written to a wide range of correspondents, deepened her emotional connections to her family, friends and community, expressed her desires and attachments and helped her to craft and develop her own narrative style, and to express her self and her way of being in the world ( Hemmings, 2005 : 550). Todd's careful and painstaking scholarship focuses on the development of Wollstonecraft's writing and of her character through the letters, detailing her sense of her own intellectual progress, and drawing out the value and power of the imagination within human relationships. I take as my starting point Todd's claim that the opinions expressed in the letters about the complexities of women's lives are ‘more revolutionary and distinctive’ than some of the ideas in the published work ( Todd, 2003 : xxviii–xxix). I argue that the ideas and preoccupations of her letters are crucial for understanding not only her character and her literary development but also her political thinking, in particular about independence, family and property. This is a contribution to the wider feminist project of tracing the prehistory of ‘the shifting fortunes of the feminist mantra that “the personal is political”’ ( Pedwell and Whitehead, 2012 : 118) and of thinking ‘both more modestly and more widely about what counts as politics’ ( Cvetkovich, 2012 : 133). Wollstonecraft's personal writings tell us a great deal about ‘home’, in Avtah Brah's sense of a ‘homing desire’, a sense of belonging, ‘a “returning” that is also “going forward”’ ( Brah, 2022 : 95). It is in this space of returning and going forward that this article meets Wollstonecraft's feminism and connects her to the present.

The letters’ focus on debt and emotional labour ties Wollstonecraft very directly to our current contexts of austerity, downward mobility and economic hardship. By drawing on her letters, we find Wollstonecraft engaging with the ‘stuff of everyday life’ ( Hall, 2023 : 33), and in particular the fragilities and anxieties of property. This connects her to a different feminist sensibility than the usual preoccupation with her liberal or republican sympathies, or the equality/difference debate. <sup>1</sup> Instead, in her letters we read about the carrying of past experiences, the effects of financial strain and the socio-economic entanglements and responsibilities that structure women's lives and possible futures. Her letters give us some access to her experience of self and tell us about her engagement with the world. Wollstonecraft wrote letters from when she was a young girl for her whole life, ‘concerned to get herself across to herself’ as Todd (2003 : xii) puts it. How does she get herself across to us, as feminist readers of her political theory? She emerges from them as a human being in relationship to others, not just her intellectual interlocutors but her family, friends and community. Exploring these entanglements with others, the hopes and desires that she is holding together and the inter-relational responsibilities that she carried ( Hall, 2023 : 42) helps us to understand her conceptions of moral agency, freedom and self-realisation in new feminist contexts, and in a different space. The letters show us the intimate links between ideas and lives, asking us to reflect on how emotional encounters, connections, relationships and traumas open us up to ourselves and to others ( Shildrick, 2020 : 182). I begin by tracing the wider and more modest politics that we find in her letters, in the space between public and private. I then explore four different emotional encounters, connections and relationships, starting with her letters to her friend Jane Arden about her struggles for independence. From there, I draw on the letters to George Blood and Joseph Johnson to engage with the context of her indebtedness, and her longing for the freedom of composure; her letters to her sister Eliza to explore her entanglements with her family at a moment of trauma; and her letters to Gilbert Imlay to reflect on her conceptions of autonomy in a new, romantic and grief-stricken context. I read all of these moments as emotional encounters with property, and with a wider conception of politics, that open Wollstonecraft up to us.

The article draws on ‘vulnerable feminist methods’ ( Chadwick, 2021 : 566) that focus on moments of disruption, ambivalence and failure, rather than trying to smooth the edges of Wollstonecraft's theory and get her to blend in to the traditional male philosophical canon. I approach Wollstonecraft's letters in the spirit of ‘vulnerable writing’, both hers and mine ( Gunuratam and Hamilton, 2017 : 9). My interpretation is partial, and as a source the letters carry in them the inevitability of uncertainty. We get bits and pieces of the story, episodic, momentary connections ‘that resonate in timbre and passion’ between the author and the reader ( Moss and Besio, 2019 : 320). As Pamela Moss and Kathryn Besio argue about reading autobiography, we are meeting the author ‘in a space that sets up for the author to be vulnerable, and for the reader to be compassionate’ ( Moss and Besio, 2019 : 320). This vulnerability and compassion generate approaches to Wollstonecraft's political ideas about freedom, gender and property that differ from the canonical conversation. My approach also engages with the ‘discomfort’ of Wollstonecraft. She writes about her own ‘still life’, being hunted down by cares and sorrow until she feels her body and soul fading away. Her letters are about debt and dependence, and are full of anxiety, tension, suspense and dread. She is an unsettling figure, often clearly unlikable, and studying her can be ‘an affective “hot-spot” of swirling feelings, failed connections and discomfort’ ( Chadwick, 2021 : 567). In her letters, we are forced to engage with her waywardness, and with what Todd (2003 : xiv) calls her ‘unflagging intensity’, her misfortunes and vexations. Wollstonecraft reminds us that we have to take seriously the toll that taking care of others and of ourselves can take on us. She is not always an easy companion, and her battles with the world and with her mental health remind us that freedom is a constant struggle.

## The letters as political theory

Wollstonecraft is a complicated, canonical figure in philosophy, English literature, feminism and the history of political thought. She wrote conduct books, treatises on education, novels, reviews, feminist philosophy, her own work of history and a ground-breaking travel memoir. In all her writings, across genres, she ‘challenges the confinement of politics to the sphere of statecraft, and the exclusion of intimate and domestic relationships from the field of political concern’ ( Frazer, 2020 : 26). We can extend her challenge by bringing her personal letters into the field of political concern, and integrating the ‘scandal’ of her life into her political theory in new and more compassionate ways ( Moore, 1999 : 1). Her personal letters create a direct and personal connection between the letter writer and her reader, a relationship of vulnerability and compassion that feels like friendship but is tinged with voyeurism, and with the judgements that we make in the present (of the past, and of Wollstonecraft's feminism). The supposed transparency of the letter form means that readers feel that they have been given ‘a direct and intimate knowledge of the writer's authentic emotions’, and even ‘direct access to selfhood’ ( McInnes, 2020 : 13). At the same time, the letters are performative, with Wollstonecraft experimenting with various self-representations, trying to create an imaginative space ‘to explore and develop new models of female agency’ ( McInnes, 2020 : 15). Eighteenth-century letters were spaces of communication, conversation and argument. As Todd points out, they were not written in the expectation of complete privacy between the writer and the intended recipient. They were kept, copied and ‘handed around among coteries or colleague groups’ ( Todd, 2003 : x), and returned to the sender when the recipient died or the relationship broke down. Correspondence was understood as ‘good conversation’, and as an entertaining and informative literary form with a potential audience of family and friends.

Women's letters were part of an epistolary culture within which women declared their sentiments to others, but also constructed, conveyed and internalised their identities through their performance of letter-writing. Letters were, as Soile Ylivouri (2018 : 9) argues, part of a complex process of negotiating feminine identities, often in the ‘liminal space between public and private spheres’. The letter held an ambiguous status between the public and the private, bringing the outside in, and sending ideas, thoughts, struggles and opinions out into the world. In their personal correspondence, women were allowed to express their ideas without ‘transgressing the boundaries of […] class and gender’ ( Ylivouri, 2018 : 150). They could be subversive. As a moral philosopher, Wollstonecraft was at home in this liminal space between the private and the public. She argued that individual struggles and failures to act morally had to be understood against the background of a society's political institutions, norms and resources ( Halldenius, 2019 ). This is part of what allows her to speak so directly to our present political contexts of austerity, downward mobility and economic hardship. Her arguments for equal representation recognise that inequalities of rank and power mitigate against the liberty of the whole community ( Halldenius, 2019 ). To find Wollstonecraft's politics in her writings, we have to dig about in her discussions of manners and morals, virtue, education, marriage and socialisation. As Elizabeth Frazer (2020 : 31) puts it, for Wollstonecraft, ‘[p]olitics explains who does and who does not have power, authority, and material and symbolic goods, and is the key to revolution or reform’. Wollstonecraft's relation to dissenting religious circles, with their dangerous discussions of equality, constitutions and sedition, made ‘politics […] inescapable, non-optional’ ( Frazer, 2020 : 32), and this inescapable politics circulates in her letters as well. In there too is practical expression of her understanding of liberty as ‘equal interdependence’ ( Halldenius, 2019 : 186). Women, she argued in the _Vindication_ , would become free ‘by being enabled to earn their own subsistence, independent of men; I mean, to prevent misconstruction, as one man is independent of another’ ( Wollstonecraft, 1995 : 260). This kind of independence underpinned the possibility of true companionship, and of equal citizenship, and so of equal interdependence.

The letters to her family and friends help to show how this interdependence was built on the fundamental premise that we need each other. Like her other writings, her letters expand the meaning of politics, and her voice is that of a political actor in a domestic space: ‘in thinking, arguing, writing, and publishing, she was engaging in politics – she was doing it’ ( Frazer, 2020 : 48). She was doing politics in her personal correspondence too. In her letters about caring for others and her own struggles with her mental health, we can see the tensions involved in this politics, between engaging with the throng of other people and seeking a ‘still life’ of her own. The letters are about the complications of getting by, and what it means to try and live as if you are free, using radical imagination and wayward practices ( Hartman, 2019 : xiii). In particular, by focusing on her experience of losing her inheritance, of trying to extricate her sister from her marriage and of being abandoned by her lover, we can trace how property functioned as a system of power in her life and the lives of those around her. As Catherine Packham (2020 : 212) argues, the patterns of inheritance and the interconnected legal systems in the 18th century spun ‘a web of power in which women were caught’. Through her letters about her struggles with the world, the trials of managing a family, the desire to subsist without being dependent on the caprice of a fellow creature and the ‘debts that I would give the world to pay’ (To George Blood, August 25 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 76), Wollstonecraft anticipates a feminist vision of justice that requires a living wage, a home, decent work and affordable childcare to be realised.

Her writings are an integral part of the ideological histories that construct freedom as both modern and universal. Her voice, as a white English and European woman from the late 18th century, is filtered through her positioning as ‘unfree’ in relation to modern feminists, but ‘free’ in relation to the enslaved and the colonised people, and to the servants, who were her contemporaries. The arresting image of her sleeping outside her mother's door draws attention to her unfreedom, and changes the images that we rely on to ‘make sense’ of her, allowing us to begin to make new connections and relations ( Youngblood Jackson, 2017 ). Wollstonecraft is fixed in many people's imaginations as the mother of Mary Shelley, making her the forebear of something extraordinary and exceptional. The story of Mary Wollstonecraft's death as a result of complications after childbirth is sometimes told as if she achieved some strange kind of resurrection in Frankenstein, and was given another chance at fame and immortality through her daughter. This is her public story of redemption, an escape route from her debts and the complications of her private life. It can be an overpowering narrative, and one that leaves out her eldest daughter Fanny, Gilbert Imlay's child. Fanny comes back to life in Wollstonecraft's personal letters which are full of anger and sadness, but also of love and care and hopes for the future and the next generation invested in Fanny, ‘our little darling’ (To Gilbert Imlay, 11 April 1795, in Wardle, 1979 : 286), the ‘little squirrel’ (To Gilbert Imlay, 15 January 1795, in Todd, 2003 : 279). After Fanny died by suicide in 1816, William Godwin wanted her left in ‘the obscurity she so much desired’ and no one went to Swansea to claim her body or to bury her ( Hindle, 2006 : 334). She was kept private, but never brought home, or returned: ‘Go not to Swansea’, Godwin wrote to Percy Shelley, ‘we will say that she has gone to Ireland, to her aunt’. In spite of being shown great care and affection by Godwin and the family during her life, Fanny was abandoned to an unknown pauper's grave ( Hindle, 2006 : 344). The newspaper account of her suicide in the _Cambrian_ tells us that her stockings were marked with the letter ‘G’, and her stays (a fully boned lace bodice) with the letters M. W. ( Hindle, 2006 : 333). This encounter with Fanny's stays is a haunting reminder that in reading Wollstonecraft's letters and engaging with the stuff of everyday life, we are also responding to the dead, 'the uncared-for dead’ and the ‘deliberately un-named’ who sometimes refuse to settle or to be silenced ( Brace, 2019 ; Shildrick, 2020 : 171, 173). The letters are full of orphans and abandoned children, people without support and a ‘home’. Fanny's misery, loneliness and pain are an affective counterpoint to the ultimately redemptive narrative of Wollstonecraft as the mother of Mary Shelley. Encountering Wollstonecraft sleeping outside her mother's door and inscribed in the stays that her elder daughter wore means finding her at the heart of stories about grief, family, home and property that spanned the generations. These complicated feelings need to find their way into the field of political concern in order for us to understand what Wollstonecraft really meant by freedom, and by interdependence.

## The freedom of friendship and wayward independence

If we go back to Wollstonecraft's letter to Jane Arden, we find her telling her friend that her father's ungovernable temper was a source of misery to her. While she acknowledged that ‘his passions’ were seldom directed at her, she attributed her ‘lingering sickness’, her ruined health and her broken spirits to living with his violence, his misconduct and his appropriation of the ‘fortune’ that should have been settled on his children. He left her, she said, with ‘nothing to expect’ and, ‘what is worse’, dependent on a stranger (To Jane Arden, early 1780, in Todd, 2003 : 24). She made connections between the different kinds of power that he exercised over her, and made clear the interconnected ways in which he caught her in the web of property. Throughout her letters, she talks about the lingering effects of his physical violence and about the struggles that she had in engaging with the world and mixing with the throng from this position of dependence. She blamed him for taking away from her the means of living freely, and her relationship to his violence shaped her demands for women's freedom and autonomy. Wollstonecraft was acutely aware of who had and who did not have power, and of how that power could be exercised in the most intimate settings.

In her letters to her friends, Wollstonecraft reflected on the damage to her mental health and on the struggle involved in fulfilling the duties of caring for children and living independent lives. She wrote to Jane Arden about being robbed of her vivacity, and wishing only for ‘a chearful settled frame of mind’ (To Jane Arden, 17 October 1781[?], in Todd, 2003 : 27) in the face of troubles that harassed her mind and body, and misfortunes that had propelled her into ‘a kind of early old age’ ( Todd, 2003 : 27). Her choices were constrained by her financial position, made with ‘prudential motives’, and she was driven by necessity to live among strangers, where she was not always comfortable (To Jane Arden, early 1780, in Todd, 2003 : 21). She talked about being ‘a piece of still life’: ‘I would move in a small circle; - I am fond of domestic pleasures and have not spirit sufficient to bustle about’ ( Todd, 2003 : 21). At the same time, she urged her friend not to be intimidated by small difficulties, to ‘struggle with any obstacles rather than go into a state of dependance’ (To Jane Arden, April 1781, in Todd, 2003 : 29). She presented herself through her letters to her friends and family, and to herself, as a woman who struggled through difficulties, exerting herself with vigour and preparing for every eventuality, determined to do what was necessary to ‘live independent or not live at all’ (To Eliza Bishop, late 1790, in Todd, 2003 : 183). This determination to live independently meant, for her, building a life with her childhood friend, the botanical illustrator Fanny Blood. <sup>2</sup> Wollstonecraft wrote to Jane Arden in 1780 that she had resolved to give up ‘every expectation and dependance’ that would interfere with spending time with Fanny ( Todd, 2003 : 30). She acknowledged that ‘this resolution may appear a little extraordinary’ ( Todd, 2003 : 30), but this was part of her wayward practice, her struggle to create an autonomous and beautiful life and live as if she was free ( Hartman, 2019 : xiii). Her ‘transformative intellectual friendship’ with Fanny ( Cronin and Botting, 2014 : 309) shaped Wollstonecraft's life and her sense of herself. She called Fanny ‘her best earthly comfort’ ( Todd, 2003 : 68) and declared that ‘this connexion must give the colour to my future days’ ( Todd, 2003 : 30). In choosing Fanny, she insisted that her resolution followed the dictates of reason and the bent of her own inclination, and so expressed her independence and autonomy and her aversion ‘to any matrimonial tie’ ( Todd, 2003 : 30).

In her letters, Wollstonecraft stressed what women's independence looked like, the decisions and choices that it involved. She also conveyed a strong sense of being subjected to the scrutiny of others. Writing to Jane Arden about visiting Windsor in the summer of 1780, she disapproved of Mrs Dawson's sister who was ‘rather too fond of dissipation’ and brought up her daughters to make them accomplished and fashionable rather than ‘good and sensible’. In doing so, she ‘follows the crowd, and it is much to be lamented, that the Stream runs so rapidly that way’ (To Jane Arden, June–August 1780, in Wardle, 1979 : 74). Wollstonecraft positioned herself as outside the stream for spending less time and energy on her appearance: ‘I believe I am thought a very poor creature, but to dress violently neither suits my inclination, nor power purse ’ ( Wardle, 1979 : 74). Her power and her purse were inseparable from each other, and both were key to what it meant to live free. Her infamous judgements of other women, her disapproval of their dissipation and of the triviality of their concerns were balanced by this sense of what constrained her, and her awareness of what it meant to turn her back on this ‘nasty world’ where ‘old maids are of so little consequence’ (To Jane Arden, late 1782, in Todd, 2003 : 38). Through her letters, we can see her count the cost of women's duty to live independent lives, and why civil and political rights are so vital in countering the patriarchal world. Women needed to be enabled to live the autonomous lives that suited their reason and their inclinations, to follow their earthly comforts and connections, and for them to be able to achieve freedom the world needed to change its expectations of them. She concluded, in her letter to Jane Arden in late 1782, that it ‘is a happy thing to be a mere blank, and to be able to pursue one's own whims, where they lead, without having a husband and half a hundred children at hand to teaze and controul a poor woman who wishes to be free’ ( Todd, 2003 : 38). Women needed other ways of living, and their own conceptions of freedom.

## Indebtedness and the freedom of composure

The poor woman who wished to be free sought the freedom of composure: ‘I have no hope nor do I endeavour to attain any thing but composure of mind and that I expect to attain in some degree inspite of the storms and cross winds of life’ (To Everina Wollstonecraft, December 1783, in Todd, 2003 : 42). For Wollstonecraft, her independence and her economy, the possibility of composure was undone by her debts and by the precariousness of her position: ‘My creditors have a right to do as they please with me, should I not be able to satisfy their demands’ (To George Blood, 27 February 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 66). Her indebtedness to others left her in what she called an ‘unprotected situation’, exposed to insults from her creditors which she had to bear with patience (To George Blood, 6 July 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 73). Debt brought with it dishonour and a weight of anxiety. She was aware that poverty more often raised contempt than pity, and she had to strive not to appear in need of assistance in order to achieve the respect of society ( Todd, 2003 : 73). She was, she said, almost afraid to look forward, and she felt oppressed by continual anxiety: ‘'tis this suspence, this dread of I cannot tell what, which harasses me’ (To George Blood, 27 February 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 67). In a letter to George Blood, Fanny's brother, in 1786, she talked about her debts inhabiting the same space as her grief for Fanny, who had died in childbirth in Portugal the year before: ‘My debts too haunt me like furies’ (To George Blood, 1 May 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 69). Grief and debt were both part of a story of loss, shock, lost connections and the possibility of being undone ( Holman Jones and Adams, 2014 ). This was another emotional encounter with property, with freedom as a constant struggle and with the interplay of independence and interdependence. Wollstonecraft planned to take cheap lodgings and live without a servant to maintain herself: ‘I have done with all worldly pursuits, or wishes’, she told George Blood: ‘I only desire to subsist, without being dependant on the caprice of an fellow creature’ (To George Blood, 22 May 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 70). In thinking about Wollstonecraft's unfreedom and the composure that she yearned for, it is important not to sidestep this moment of making a choice to ‘live without a servant’ and the sense of sacrifice, and retreat from the world, that she implied it involved. Her financial situation, and presumably her vision of subsistence, was different from that of the servant who ‘maintained’ her.

Wollstonecraft was alert to the ways in which ‘class differentiates and stratifies women's experiences of patriarchal, gender-based inequality and oppression’ ( Botting, 2019 : 367), and her novel _Maria: Or, The Wrongs of Woman_ of 1798 (Wollstonecraft, 2009a) compares the experiences of the middle-class Maria and her servant Jemima. She made it clear that Jemima was bound more tightly by the web of property, and had less than Maria in terms of both material and symbolic goods. Wollstonecraft's own financial situation was often precarious, but her rage against her father was about having lost something, having her rightful expectations of social mobility taken away from her. The sense of loss of status and security that pervades her letters reflects the threat that her father's profligacy posed to her property, to her freedom and to her future. She found herself thrust out into contingency, and unable to exercise enforceable property rights. As a middle-class white woman, she was supposed to have her expectations confirmed, legitimated and protected by the law ( Harris, 1993 : 1713). Part of her wider argument for a revolution in manners was about what should count as reasonable expectations for women, and therefore merit the protection of the law as forms of property. She recognised property as ‘a legal construct by which selected private interests are protected and upheld’, and argued for the redrawing of boundaries to reorder the existing regime of power ( Harris, 1993 : 1730). She was opposed to aristocratic property, excessive wealth and unequal inheritance, the ways in which property was arbitrarily exercised as domination over others. She saw the inequalities that were supported by the law as ‘conscious selections regarding the structuring of social relations’ ( Harris, 1993 : 1730). At the same time, she was not opposed to property in all its manifestations, and Susan Ferguson (1999) argues that her ideal was a moderately wealthy family that could sustain itself in comfort, with some funds for charity and buying books. Such an ideal required a female servant, and so, Ferguson argues, there was an assumption of class exploitation in Wollstonecraft's theory of property ( Ferguson, 1999 : 441). Race as well as class is invented through property, and the freedom of composure she was claiming for herself shared with whiteness the ‘conceptual nucleus’ of the ‘right to exclude’ ( Harris, 1993 : 1714). Once we bring this into our field of concern, there are glimpses in her letters of her own domination over others, and we have to remember her place as a white woman who aspired to employ a servant in the colonial matrix of power and within a ‘kaleidoscopic constellation of multiple flows of power’ ( Brah, 2022 : 83). She resisted the idea of becoming a governess because ‘I should be shut out of society – and be debarred the _imperfect_ pleasures of friendship – as I should be on every side surrounded by _unequals_ ’ (To George Blood, 6 July 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 74; emphasis in original). Under such circumstances, she expected little in terms of kindness or closeness to others.

In considering what Wollstonecraft meant by autonomy and independence, and the context in which she made demands for women's freedom, the moments of wishing to find a way through precarity, to find a space of peace and quiet, where it was possible to give up ‘bustling forever with the world’ (To George Blood, 22 May 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 70), are significant. Wollstonecraft wrote to George Blood in 1786, ‘I must be independent and earn my own subsistence, or be very uncomfortable’ (To George Blood, 18 June 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 71). She told her sister Everina in November 1787 that she was determined not to see any friends until she was earning her own subsistence (To Everina, 7 November 1787, in Todd, 2003 : 139). This was the discomfort not only of poverty but also of disappointment and of being the object of pity and advice. In Wollstonecraft's thought, independence, virtue and equality fit together and are inseparable from one another. Dependence risked the corruption of virtue through manipulation, and it undermined the delicate balance of equality. The weakness that makes a woman depend on a man for subsistence ‘produces a kind of cattish affection which leads a wife to purr about her husband as she would about any man who fed and caressed her’ ( Wollstonecraft, 1995 : 271). In her letters she talked about how it felt and what it meant to be obliged to others who are not your equals, to long for peace and independence: ‘Every obligation we receive from our fellow-creatures is a new shackle, takes us from our native freedom, and debases the mind, makes us mere earthworms – I am not fond of grovelling!’ (To Joseph Johnson, 13 September 1787, in Todd, 2003 : 134). The burden of her debts was not only financial. There is a strong sense in her letters of how they wear away your freedom and leave you feeling that you are living a life that no longer belongs to you ( Carraway, 2019 ). Her pecuniary affairs tormented her, and she talked about how her debts plagued her, ‘ _these trifles_ buzz continually in my ears’ (To George Blood, 6 October 1791, in Todd, 2003 : 188; emphasis in original). In the shadows of the letters, Wollstonecraft was haunted by debt and inequality and these experiences lived alongside her grief for Fanny Blood. Together they helped to teach her what independence meant for women who were not fond of grovelling to others. ‘There is certainly a great defect in my mind’, Wollstonecraft wrote to Joseph Johnson in 1792, ‘my wayward heart creates its own misery’ (To Joseph Johnson, October 1792 [?], in Todd, 2003 : 206).

## Family entanglements

As part of re-encountering Wollstonecraft through her letters, and thinking about her feminist commitments, her waywardness and her relevance for today's feminism, I focus now on the episode in 1784 when she helped her sister Eliza to separate from her husband, leaving her baby behind. It is an instance of trauma and emotional connection which tells us a great deal about the complications of the homing desire, the entanglements of family life and the possible futures for women in the late 18th century. The letters show us Wollstonecraft's struggles with inter-relational responsibilities, with her sense of moral agency and with the idea of freedom. Her sister Eliza suffered severe depression after the birth of her baby daughter. It was vividly described by Wollstonecraft as a ‘disorder’ that resulted in fits of frenzy and left her mind ‘in a most unsettled state’, with her ideas ‘all disjointed’; wild whims ‘float on her imagination and unconnected fall from her’ (To Everina Wollstonecraft, late 1783, in Todd, 2003 : 39). Wollstonecraft's letters to her other sister, Everina, described what it was like to care for Eliza in this state, how ‘harassing’ it was to attend to her constant fluctuations and to be forced to keep an eye on her all the time. Eliza's distress and the burdens of care that it placed on Wollstonecraft added another layer to being kept in suspense and to losing the freedom of composure (for both Mary and Eliza). She talked about turning over ‘the unaccountable wanderings of [Eliza's] mind’ to try and make sense of them, finding herself always dwelling on the same topic, worrying about whether her sister would ever regain her faculties ( Todd, 2003 : 39–40). There is a real resonance in these descriptions of what it is like to care for others close to you, to be caught between feeling unable to stay ‘and see this continual misery’ and feeling unable to ‘leave her to bear it by herself without any one to comfort her’ ( Todd, 2003 : 40). Wollstonecraft expressed frustration at trying to lead or govern a weak mind, at being ‘hurried with listening to pros and cons and my head is so confused’, her heart almost broken with listening ( Todd, 2003 : 42). To build a feminism that focuses on how to demand freedom and self-realisation, we need to listen carefully, and with compassion, to Wollstonecraft talking about the stresses and strains of what it means to care for others in a situation where women are unprotected, interdependent and vulnerable.

Wollstonecraft took Eliza away from her husband, a move which she recognised was a ‘shocking affair of a woman's leaving her bed-fellow’ ( Todd, 2003 : 47). She hoped that ‘our poor girl will never again be in this man's power’ ( Todd, 2003 : 48), and went into hiding with her in Hackney. She found Eliza was more composed than she expected, but Wollstonecraft reported to Everina that she ‘was afraid in the coach that she was going to have one of her flights for she bit her _wedding ring_ to pieces’ ( Todd, 2003 : 43; emphasis in original). She conveyed the anxiety of hiding from Eliza's husband, keeping secrets and trying to support her sister who ‘looks now very wild’ ( Todd, 2003 : 44). Wollstonecraft positioned herself as rescuer, trying to ‘snatch Bess <sup>3</sup> from extreme wretchedness’, worrying about leaving her daughter (Elizabeth Mary, born in 1783), since mothers had no legal custody of their children if they separated from their husbands. It was a moment of waywardness, of refusal to be governed ( Hartman, 2019 : xv), by both Eliza and Mary. Todd describes the rescue plan as ‘impudent and imprudent’, with Mary knowing that she was rendering Elizabeth motherless but pushing on without consideration of the baby's best interests, putting herself centre stage in the drama ( Todd, 2000 : 46–49). It was also a moment of desperation that shows us how both sisters were caught in the web of power, property and the law. Meredith Bishop's interests as a husband and father were protected and upheld against his wife, and his expectations were confirmed and legitimated, while she was forced into dependence on others. Baby Elizabeth died just before her first birthday and never saw her mother again. Bishop never divorced Eliza, and as Todd concludes: ‘If she wished to retain any remnants of reputation, there was no question of any further relationship with a man’ ( Todd, 2000 : 57). Eliza, even more than her sister, was caught in the space of unpayable debts, lost connections and grief. After the initial trauma/drama, the problem, then as now, was the need for a ‘separate maintenance’ or the possibility of earning her own bread. They needed to find a way of engaging with the world that allowed them to look after each other. Wollstonecraft was trying to work out how she and her sisters and Fanny Blood could form a household, or an ‘all-female family’ ( Botting, 2019 : 368). A school was out of the question because ‘the MONIES did not answer’ (To Everina Wollstonecraft, January 1784, in Todd, 2003 : 46). She suggested that ‘with economy’, they could get by on a guinea a week which they could earn for themselves ‘and we shall be independant [sic]’ ( Todd, 2003 : 46). Mrs C, identified by Todd as Mrs Clare, gave them cautious words of approval, offered to lend them money and sent them a pie and a bottle of wine, as well as coming to visit them in a coach through the rain ( Todd, 2003 : 50). Hannah Burgh persuaded Wollstonecraft to start the coeducational day school in Newington Green and provided financial backing, along with her husband James Burgh, from 1784 to 1788 ( Gordon, 2005 : 69). ‘We are good oeconomists’, said Wollstonecraft ( Todd, 2003 : 49), though she was racking her brains to come up with a feasible plan.

In these social spaces of economy and interdependence, between precarity and possibility, women helped each other to survive and to try to live free. Even with this network of support, the attempt to build a household, to remake their lives, was short-lived and hard work, full of labour and sorrow (To George Blood, 14 August 1785, Todd, 2003 : 60). Wollstonecraft confided in Everina that she had borrowed 20 pounds from Eliza's husband Meredith Bishop to try and prevent the financial ruin of the Bloods. She described the distress of having to do this, even though ‘he lent it very properly without any parade’: ‘it made me miserable that I saw I was _entangling_ myself with an _obligation_ ’ (To Everina Wollstonecraft, January 1784, Todd, 2003 : 46; emphasis in original). Now she was hoping for a ‘little present of furniture’ from her brother, ‘but if he is prudent we must try to do without it’ ( Todd, 2003 : 47). After she left for Portugal to take care of Fanny Blood, the school ‘dwindled to nothing’ while she was away. Fanny died in childbirth and Wollstonecraft had too many debts to remain in the house and pay the rent. She lived in the hope that Fanny Blood's husband, Hugh Skeys, would send money to support them, but she complained to George Blood that he ‘has no consideration’ (To George Blood, 27 February 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 67) and would ‘not run any hazard to serve a friend’ (To George Blood, 4 February 1786, in Todd, 2003 : 66). She wanted to be a mother and protector to her sisters, but felt that she was not ‘formed to obtain the good things of this world’ (To Henry Dyson Gabel, 13 September 1787, in Todd, 2003 : 135). Her letters convey a strong sense of being continually involved in various schemes, constantly straining her nerves to settle Eliza, trying to mitigate her sisters’ fate and secure them a home and refuge, working, hustling and paying people back, ‘yet while [these schemes] are pursued life slips away’ (To Everina Wollstonecraft, 7 November 1787, in Todd, 2003 : 139). This was not the freedom of composure, or the impulsiveness of a wayward life. She felt the strain of assuming responsibility for her father's financial affairs and negotiating on behalf of her sisters, trying to find them work for a salary that was not too mean, or hedged around with disagreeable conditions. She wrote to Eliza that ‘if I had not cared for my sisters who certainly do not adore me – the two last years of my life might have passed tranquilly not embittered by pecuniar [sic] cares’ (To Eliza Bishop, late 1790, in Todd, 2003 : 183). Instead, her life was ‘involved in debts’ ( Todd, 2003 : 183). Wollstonecraft's plans were immediately entangled with debt and complex transactions between different families for whom she felt responsible, and between Wollstonecraft and the men who were in a position to help her. Striving for equal interdependence was never straightforward. Through her letters, we can see the strain of inter-relational responsibilities and the limitations placed on her moral agency by the stuff of everyday life. Her constant struggle for freedom was also about her moral and economic attachment to the household, her homing desire and her commitment to improving the quality of life of the individuals within the family. It was not possible to escape the web of property and power, or men's control of both.

Her letters are full of insight into the social relations that underpin debt and dependence, the language of credit, friendship and disappointment. She wrote to George Blood to tell him how vexed she was with her brother Charles, how difficult it was to trust him or to convince him to be ‘punctual’ (To George Blood, 15 September 1789, in Todd, 2003 : 163). She described him to Joshua Cristall <sup>4</sup> in 1790 as living an unsettled, unmanly life in Cork, ‘eating the bread of idleness – and living on the kindness of relations’ (To Joshua Cristall, 19 March 1790, in Todd, 2003 : 168). This was a gendered portrait of property relations. Charles benefited from the injustices of the property system and the accumulation of wealth, as well as from Britain's imperialism and from his sister's efforts on his behalf to weave him into the web of power. His relationship to property, freedom and waywardness was very different from hers. He was her opposite in many ways, not a good economist, not striving to be independent, but unsettled and thoughtless. In October 1791, Wollstonecraft wrote to William Roscoe <sup>5</sup> about Charles that ‘[i]f he remains much longer idle, he will, of course, grow vicious. His boiling blood could only be cooled by employment and I cannot procure him a situation’ (To William Roscoe, 6 October 1791, in Todd, 2003 : 191). She was frustrated and repeatedly disappointed in trying to get him an appointment in the East India service, and was writing to Roscoe to try and procure him a station in a counting house in Liverpool, at the heart of the empire. We get sight of how Wollstonecraft and her family were imbricated in the colonial matrix of power. Her conceptions of what it means to be respectable, to find a home in the world and a sense of belonging are caught up with the everyday life of empire. The politics of her brother's location, and his mobility, enmeshed him in the modalities of power in ways that highlighted his access to freedom and enforceable property rights. At the same time, his own lack of moral agency and inter-relational responsibility, his refusal of equal interdependence, meant that Charles was a constant weight on her mind, a ‘blister’ ( Todd, 2003 : 191). In 1792 she arranged for him to go to America with a couple, Joel and Ruth Barlow, who would try and find him a place on a farm to gain some experience until he could buy some land for himself. It was, she felt, up to her to settle him as a man of property and substance. She hoped this scheme would ‘put him in the way to be settled’ (To William Roscoe, 3 January 1792, in Todd, 2003 : 195) and give him the chance to ‘earn a respectable livelihood if he will exert himself’ (To William Roscoe, 14 February 1792, in Todd, 2003 : 196). His waywardness was more containable than hers, and in the end his interests would be protected and upheld. His relationship to property was different from hers, allowing him to live and survive as an independent being, to maintain his expectations and to insulate himself from the world.

## Imlay and the sea of trouble

In this final section, I bring Wollstonecraft’s anguished love letters to Gilbert Imlay <sup>6</sup> into this same field of political concern of her demands for freedom and autonomy, and what it means to live free. In her pain and estrangement from her lover, we can see the gendered ways in which men and women were caught in the web of property, and the different meanings of independence that they were pursuing. In the same way as Charles never really finds himself in an unprotected situation, and could be settled by his own exertions, Imlay's relationship to property and commerce was different from hers. As Andrew Cayton (2015 : 124) points out, Gilbert Imlay embodied a particular American version of masculinity, tied to independence, defiance and freedom from constraint. His focus was on acquiring reputation and fortune, and on experimenting with freedom. Like Wollstonecraft, he lived a life that was involved with debt, but his relationship to investment and speculation was very different and he rejected the local world of the household ( Cayton, 2015 : 126). His focus was on getting ahead, mixing in the transatlantic throng of publishers, merchants and politicians and using his masculinised agency to move around the world. Her despairing, desperate letters to him after he had left her behind talk about the original, modest plans he had shared with her to gain a thousand pounds and procure a farm in America, ‘which would have been an independence’ (To Gilbert Imlay, 10 February 1795, in Todd, 2003 : 282). This imagined future had clearly aligned with her values, but then she found that Imlay changed. He discovered that ‘a certain situation is life is more necessary to you than you imagined – more necessary than an uncorrupted heart’ ( Todd, 2003 : 282). All those promises of independence had been broken in his desertion of her, his failure to provide protection or security, to bring composure or quiet to her life. She had, she wrote to Imlay in 1795, certain principles of action which contradicted his and which grounded her more feminist narrative of property. She would not found her happiness on money, but would focus on exerting herself to obtain the necessaries of life for her child, earning enough for their subsistence and refusing to be under a pecuniary obligation to Imlay while he was neglecting them. As ever for Wollstonecraft, debt was linked to dignity, and independence to virtue, but still shot through with class: ‘I would sooner submit to menial service. – I wanted the support of your affection – that gone, all is over!’ (To Gilbert Imlay, 9 February 1795, in Todd, 2003 : 281). Her refusal to be teased or controlled resurfaces here,
